profile.name: Delbin
profile.availability: Open to work — full-time, part-time, contract (3–6 months) & freelance
profile.bio: I'm Delbin (Toe Htet Arkar), a Product Designer with 4 years of experience turning complex product ideas into intuitive user interfaces. I specialize in AI design and enterprise solutions, and I've worked across synthetic data & computer vision, defense training systems, healthcare, and edtech — usually owning the work from first research through final UI. Bilingual (EN / MM), based in Bangkok with remote experience across Singapore, Indonesia, California, and Myanmar.

caseStudies.title: The math wasn't the problem.
caseStudies.blurb: Grade 4 students kept stalling on Olympiad questions they could actually solve. Unscripted observation found three blockers — language, attention, navigation. None were math.

detail.subtitle: Everything around it was.
detail.summary: Grade 4 students kept stalling on Olympiad questions they could actually solve. We ran unscripted observations on a live competitor platform and found three blockers — language, attention, and navigation. None of them were about math. That finding reshaped every design decision that followed — including killing a visual theme the client already loved.

items.label: Team
items.value: Split team — I owned the student experience

items.label: Users observed
items.sub: 5 Grade 4 students and 3 guardians, on a live competitor platform

items.label: Modules tested
items.sub: Challenge, Training, Class — same problems in all three

items.label: Visual directions
items.sub: Built two full themes. Killed the one the client liked.

sections.title: Kids weren't failing the math. They were failing the interface.
paragraphs: We watched five students use a competitor platform. Every module told the same story: language got in the way, long videos lost them, and navigation was built for adults. When we translated the questions, they performed well. The barrier was the wrapper, not the work.

items.label: Problem
items.text: Kids 6–13 were disengaging from Olympiad math. Nobody could say why with evidence.

items.label: Method
items.text: Remote unscripted observation of 5 students + 3 guardians on KooBits. Scored on behaviour, time, and confidence.

items.label: Decision
items.text: Design for behaviour, not age. Strip language friction, shorten everything, separate learning from play.

items.label: Surfaces
items.text: Three modules rebuilt around familiar metaphors: a staircase, a book, a noticeboard.

items.label: Outcome
items.text: A calmer, clearer student app — and a theme pivot that saved the product from its own polish.

items.label: Lesson
items.text: A design that wins the room can still lose the child. Test the theme, not just the flow.

sections.title: Smart kids, stuck.
sections.subtitle: The brief was simple. The kids weren't.
paragraphs: An EdTech client wanted to bring Olympiad-level math to Myanmar's 6-to-13s — a local KooBits, but playful and built for children who'd never used an interface like it. The hard part was never the curriculum. It was that nobody could explain, with evidence, why children stalled. Was it the math? The reading? The screen itself?

sections.text: I didn't want to design for a six-year-old in theory. I wanted to watch one actually get stuck.

sections.title: Five students. Three modules. One method.
sections.subtitle: Before drawing a single screen, we watched real kids fail in real time.
paragraphs: We ran remote, unscripted observations with five Grade 4 students and three guardians, using KooBits as a live stand-in. Unscripted on purpose — the moment you give a child a script, you stop seeing how they actually behave. We tested three modules: Challenge, Training, and Class, scoring every session through three lenses.

notes.title: Usage behaviour
notes.text: Could they use the interface without help? How much cognitive load did it cost?

notes.title: Time taken
notes.text: Challenge 1:27 · Training 1:46 · Class 0:40

notes.title: Confidence
notes.text: Rated "Not Much" across all three modules.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Child testing the competitor platform ]

sections.text: Every module told the same story in a different accent.

sections.title: Three blockers. None of them were math.
sections.subhead: The same problems showed up in Challenge, Training, and Class.

items.title: Language barrier
items.text: Students struggled to translate questions. But when we translated for them, they performed well. The math wasn't hard — the English was.

items.title: Attention
items.text: Long tutorial videos lost them every time. Most never watched the solution explanations. If it wasn't short, it didn't exist.

items.title: Navigation + dependence
items.text: Students needed guardian help for basic tasks. Navigation was built for adults — kids couldn't find their way through alone.

sections.title: Teachers hit the same walls.
paragraphs: We also got teacher feedback on the existing platform: onboarding screens weren't clear, reports were confusing, and one teacher couldn't find the quiz after finishing a tutorial.

sections.text: When the questions were translated, they could do the math. The interface was the barrier, not the content.

sections.title: Two apps. I owned one.
sections.subtitle: With the problem defined, the team split.
paragraphs: A parallel group took the parent app — progress tracking, subscriptions, oversight. I took the student experience. I mapped how a child actually moves through the platform — log in, pick a class, watch a short video, take a quiz — and built a user flow and information architecture simple enough for a six-year-old to hold in their head.
The research gave us clear rules: strip language friction, keep everything short, and separate learning content from gamified content so neither dilutes the other.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: User flow ]
[ IMAGE: Information architecture ]

sections.title: Three modules, three metaphors.
sections.subtitle: Low-fi first — because the cheapest place to be wrong is a wireframe.
paragraphs: Wireframes came before anything polished. They surfaced three rules fast: navigation needed large, tappable targets; learning and play had to be visibly separated; and mobile was its own design problem — not a shrunk desktop.
Each module then borrowed a metaphor kids already understood.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Low-fi wireframes — desktop ]
[ IMAGE: Low-fi wireframes — mobile ]

items.title: Challenge — a staircase.
items.text: Progress you can see. Completed steps, locked steps, and retries laid out as a climb. Practice feels like going up, not in circles.

items.title: Class — a book.
items.text: Linear and calm. Short video, then a quiz, then the next. Content broken into pieces small enough to survive a short attention span.

items.title: Training — a noticeboard.
items.text: HOTS and Singapore Math, organised by proficiency and high scores. Chalkboard styling that feels warm, not clinical.

sections.title: The theme everyone loved was the wrong one.
sections.subtitle: We built a beautiful direction, then killed it.
paragraphs: The first visual concept was a space-exploration theme — cosmic visuals, glowing gradients, futuristic UI. The client liked it immediately. Then we built it out, and the truth surfaced: too complex, and it pulled against the core promise of a calm, pressure-free place to learn.

So we pivoted to a second direction — cleaner, brighter, friendlier. Less spectacle, more clarity. It was the harder call because the first version was likeable. But "likeable in a review" and "right for the user" aren't the same thing.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Space theme — initial version ]
[ IMAGE: Final version ]

sections.text: The space theme won the room. It would have lost the child.

sections.title: What this project taught me.

items.title: Design for behaviour, not age.
items.text: "Six-to-thirteen" isn't a spec. Short attention, limited reading, and a need for visual clarity are the real constraints — and they only surface when you watch real children.

items.title: The barrier is usually the wrapper.
items.text: The kids could do the math. Language, video length, and navigation were doing the failing. Fixing the frame beat simplifying the content.

items.title: Align stakeholders before high-fi.
items.text: Sharing research, flows, and wireframes before polished screens meant the theme pivot was a course-correction, not a crisis.

items.title: A loved design can still be wrong.
items.text: The space theme proved client enthusiasm is a signal, not a verdict. The user is the verdict.

sections.title: If I could go back.
paragraphs: I'd test the visual theme the same way we tested the flow — cheaply, with real kids, before building it out fully. We caught the navigation issues through observation but let the space theme run all the way to a finished design before discovering it didn't fit. A rough themed prototype in front of five children would have caught that in an afternoon, not a sprint.

caseStudies.paletteLabel: Prove the model before you trust the camera.
caseStudies.title: Prove the model before you trust the camera.
caseStudies.metrics: Vision AI Ops · Sigmawave AI · May–Jul 2026
caseStudies.blurb: Accel takes a computer vision model from “trained” to “trusted” — build the model and its rules, validate against real footage, deploy only what passes, then monitor it running live. A full-stack, on-premise Vision AI operations platform I designed end-to-end.
caseStudies.mockLabel: [ Accel — Run Analysis result: score, activity log, VLM reasoning ]

detail.summary: Accel is where a computer vision model goes from “trained” to “trusted.” Operators build a model and the rules it runs on, validate it against real footage, deploy only what passes to cameras across their sites, then monitor it running live — all in one on-premise platform they fully own. I designed the whole thing: model and rule creation, the validation pipeline, the deployment layer, the live monitoring wall, and the admin spine that governs it. The promise built into every screen: complete operator autonomy from day one.
detail.subtitle: Designing Accel — a Vision AI operations platform for defence and security operators.

items.label: Build → Validate → Deploy → Monitor
items.sub: Assemble a model and rules, prove it against real footage, deploy only what passes, then watch it work live

items.label: Platform sections
items.sub: Monitor, Manage, Deploy, System, and Profile — one operator loop across 17 feature modules

items.label: On-prem platform
items.sub: Full-stack, operator-owned, no dependence on a vendor cloud to run Vision AI

sections.title: A model that scores well can still fail on a real camera.
paragraphs: A computer vision model can pass every benchmark and still miss a person in bad light, misread an unfamiliar angle, or flag a shadow as a threat. In real deployment those failures surface late — on a live camera, when remediation is expensive. Accel closes that gap. Operators build a model and its rules, validate it against real footage, deploy only validated models to cameras, and monitor them at work — without stitching together five different tools to do it. My job was to design every surface in that loop and make a genuinely complex operations platform feel like one coherent path.

items.label: Problem
items.text: Models get deployed on trust, not proof. Validation is fragmented across tools, and defining detection logic usually needs an engineer.

items.label: Method
items.text: Designed around one operator loop — build, validate, deploy, monitor — organised into five clear platform sections, against a three-role permission matrix.

items.label: Decision
items.text: Make Run Analysis the differentiator: a model must be validated against real footage, with VLM reasoning, before it can be deployed.

items.label: Surfaces
items.text: Model management, rules, run analysis, deployment, dashboard, live monitoring, recordings, incident reports, sites, cameras, NVRs, users, billing.

items.label: Outcome
items.text: A platform that takes a model from “built” to “validated” to “deployed and watched” — fully on-premise, fully operator-owned.

items.label: Lesson
items.text: In a complex operations tool, the hardest and most valuable design work is making the right path the obvious one.

sections.title: Models get deployed on trust, not proof.
sections.subtitle: And trust is the wrong currency for a detection model.
paragraphs: Computer vision teams working with synthetic and hybrid-synthetic data face the same three problems again and again. There's a validation gap — models that score well in testing fail in real conditions because of lighting, angle, occlusion, and environmental variation, and those failures only surface after deployment when they're expensive to fix. There's tooling fragmentation — teams stitch together separate platforms for dataset management, video testing, performance visualisation, rule definition, deployment, and monitoring, and the seams between them hide root cause and slow every iteration. And there's detection-logic accessibility — defining when a model should trigger an alert usually requires an engineer, locking out the operations and domain experts who understand the use case best.

Accel had to close all three at once, for operators in defence and security who need to run, manage, and own their Vision AI on their own infrastructure. My job was to design that unified loop — and make it something operators actually run, not something they skip under pressure.

notes.title: Validation gap
notes.text: Models that score well in testing fail in real conditions because of lighting, angle, occlusion, and environmental variation — and the failures only surface after deployment, when they're expensive to fix.

notes.title: Tooling fragmentation
notes.text: Separate platforms for datasets, video testing, performance visualisation, rule definition, deployment, and monitoring. The seams between them hide root cause and slow every iteration.

notes.title: Detection-logic accessibility
notes.text: Defining when a model should trigger an alert usually requires an engineer — locking out the operations and domain experts who understand the use case best.

sections.text: A model you haven't validated isn't a capability. It's an unproven assumption sitting on a live camera.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: The gap — model trained vs model proven on real footage ]

sections.title: One operator loop. Five sections.
sections.subtitle: Build → validate → deploy → monitor, plus the governance around it.
paragraphs: Rather than design seventeen modules as seventeen problems, I designed one loop and organised everything into five top-level sections that follow it. Operators Manage their sites, cameras, NVRs, models, and rules — building the detection logic first. They Deploy — but only after Run Analysis validates a model against footage. They Monitor the live result on a dashboard, camera wall, and incident feed. And the System and Profile sections hold the governance — users, device health, configuration, activity logs, and billing — that keeps the loop safe and accountable.

That framing settled the priorities early. It's why a model is built and its rules attached before anything else. It's why validation comes before deployment, not beside it. And it's why live monitoring is framed as the payoff of the loop, not a separate product bolted on.

sections.text: I stopped counting screens and started counting steps in one operator loop. The sections organised themselves after that.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Section map — Monitor / Manage / Deploy / System / Profile ]

sections.title: Build the model. Write the rules.
sections.subtitle: The detection logic comes first — and a non-engineer can define it.
paragraphs: Before a model can be proven or deployed, it has to be built. Model Management is where a detection model is assembled as an SOP-compliant sequence of processing steps — uploaded as JSON or .onnx files, each with an action or model label — with rules attached from the library or added inline. The result is a single model artefact: an ordered sequence of steps plus the rules that govern it, ready for validation.

The Rules Library is the detection-logic layer, and it's the answer to the “you need an engineer” problem. Operators compose rules from structured conditions, start from pre-built templates (perimeter intrusion, PPE compliance, crowd density), and every rule carries an auto-generated plain-English summary so a non-engineer can read exactly what it does before saving. Rules assign the severity that ranks detections downstream, and they attach directly to models — so the logic and the model that runs it are never separated.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Model Management — SOP sequence builder with attached rules ]
[ IMAGE: Rules Library — template-based builder with plain-English summary ]

sections.text: Rules written in plain English are how a domain expert — not just an engineer — gets to define what the system watches for.

sections.title: The MVP differentiator.
sections.subtitle: Upload footage, run the model, read the verdict — with reasoning.
paragraphs: Run Analysis is where a built model gets proven, so it earned the most design attention. The flow is deliberately linear: select a model, select a preferred SOP VLM to reason about the footage, upload a representative video clip, and run. What comes back isn't just a number — it's a score, an activity log, and a VLM reasoning narrative that describes and explains what the model saw and why it behaved the way it did. The whole run is exportable as a report.

That reasoning narrative is what makes the verdict trustworthy instead of opaque. A score alone tells an operator whether a model passed; the VLM narrative tells them why — which is what lets a domain expert, not just an ML engineer, decide if the model meets the bar. Past analyses are kept and searchable, so a model's track record — every run, score, and report — is never a mystery.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Run Analysis — select model, select SOP VLM, upload footage ]
[ IMAGE: Analysis result — score, activity log, VLM reasoning narrative, export ]

sections.text: The whole platform rests on one moment: a verdict an operator can trust. The VLM reasoning is what turns a score into a decision.

sections.title: Put the proven model on a camera.
sections.subtitle: The hinge between validation and live production.
paragraphs: Deployment is where a validated model meets real hardware. An operator maps a proven model to a specific site, area, and camera through search and filter, and it begins continuous frame-by-frame rule evaluation on the live stream. Only a model that has passed Run Analysis reaches this point — validation is what earns a model its place on a camera.

Designing this meant making “what's running where” legible at a glance. Deployment history keeps the full operational context — which model watches which camera, across which sites, deployed by whom, against which last validation reference — with direct navigation from the deployment view straight into any linked camera to edit settings or review recordings. An operator always knows exactly which proven models are live, and where.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Deploy Models — mapping a validated model to site / area / camera ]
[ IMAGE: Deployment history — what's running where, with validation trail ]

sections.text: Validation earns the right to deploy. Deployment is where that proof becomes something running on a live feed.

sections.title: Watching validated models at work.
sections.subtitle: The payoff for the whole loop — readable under pressure.
paragraphs: Once models are deployed, the Monitor section is where their work shows up. The Dashboard is the central operations screen: real-time panels for detection performance and operational events, a persistent time filter (today, 7 days, 30 days, custom) applied across every panel, and drill-down into the modules beneath. Live Monitoring is the multi-camera grid with configurable layouts and per-tile feed-health indicators, plus active/inactive camera counts. Recordings lets operators search past footage by site, area, camera, date, or keyword and play it back against a synchronised, severity-filterable event timeline.

The Detection Feed is the raw stream of what models are catching — filterable by time, site, area, severity, camera, or model, with counters for total detections, critical/high, pending triage, and escalated cases. Each detection carries its model, severity, precision and confidence scores, and location — with a clear call to action: raise it as an official Incident Report, dismiss it, or attach it to an existing report. Incident Reports are the accountable layer: auto-generated from high-severity events or created manually, moving through a Pending → Acknowledged → Resolved lifecycle, each one audit-ready, linked to exact footage timestamps, and exportable as a PDF.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Dashboard — real-time panels with persistent time filter ]
[ IMAGE: Live Monitoring — multi-camera grid with feed-health indicators ]
[ IMAGE: Detection Feed → Incident Report handoff, model + score trail ]

sections.text: A detection is only useful if it becomes accountable work. The path from “the model flagged this” to “someone owns this” had to be a couple of clicks.

sections.title: Who can do what, and whether the hardware is healthy.
sections.subtitle: Governance one layer back from the hot path.
paragraphs: Around the operator loop sits the System section, designed against a three-role matrix — Owner, Admin, User — so what each person can see and do is a deliberate decision, not an accident of routing. User Management handles email-invite onboarding, role assignment, and lifecycle actions.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: User Management — three-role matrix, invite and lifecycle actions ]

sections.title: What this project taught me.

items.title: Design the loop, not the module count.
items.text: Seventeen modules sounds like seventeen design problems. It was really one operator loop — build, validate, deploy, monitor — repeated at different altitudes. Framing it that way kept a dense platform coherent instead of sprawling.

items.title: Make the right path the only path.
items.text: Validation only protects an operator if it comes before deployment by design. Making Run Analysis the gate, not a suggestion, did more for the product's promise than any amount of persuasive UI.

items.title: A score needs a reason.
items.text: The most trustworthy thing in the validation flow is the VLM reasoning narrative. A number tells you whether a model passed; the reasoning tells you why — and that's what lets a domain expert make the call instead of an engineer.

sections.title: If I could go back.
paragraphs: I'd get the Run Analysis flow in front of real operators sooner. I designed the validation loop — footage in, score and VLM reasoning out, exportable report — mostly through my own reasoning and internal review. It holds up, but one session watching an operator validate a model they actually cared about would have surfaced friction faster than another round of my own iteration.

And I'd pin down the three-role permission matrix before designing the surfaces, not alongside them. Role rules ended up shaping nearly every screen; settling who can do what up front would have saved rework each time a “should a User see this?” question reopened a layout I'd already called done.

caseStudies.paletteLabel: A working product. A better one to build.
caseStudies.title: A working product. A better one to build.
caseStudies.blurb: Terra generates synthetic data to train AI vision models — datasets that are dangerous, expensive, or impossible to collect in the real world. A working version already existed. My job was to redesign it end-to-end: better usability, more features, and a real fix for the pain points users hit in the original.

detail.summary: Terra generates synthetic data to train AI vision models — the datasets that are dangerous, expensive, or impossible to collect in the real world. A working version already existed. My job was to redesign it: better usability, more features, and a real fix for the pain points users hit in the original. I owned it end-to-end, from competitor research to the final 3D builder.
detail.subtitle: Redesigning Terra's synthetic data platform — from research to a full 3D builder.

items.label: Workflows supported
items.sub: Visual Synthetic Data (VSD) full 3D, and Hybrid Synthetic Data (HSD) synthetic logic on real video

items.label: Feature modules
items.sub: Dashboard, org management, 3D editor, object and environment settings, timeline, generation

items.label: Export formats
items.sub: Datasets auto-annotated in COCO, YOLO, and Pascal VOC — train-ready on export

sections.title: The tool worked. It just needed to be better.
paragraphs: Terra wasn't new. A working version already shipped — but it was rigid. Scenes were hard to customise, there was no real camera control, lighting was fixed, and teams couldn't scale their work. I redesigned the whole thing: benchmarked the leading synthetic data tools, learned Blender and Unreal so I actually understood the 3D work our users did, gathered feedback from real users of the old version, and rebuilt Terra around a focused, canvas-first 3D builder.

items.label: Problem
items.text: The original Terra worked, but it was rigid: limited scene customisation, no camera control, fixed lighting, poor scalability for teams.

items.label: Method
items.text: Benchmarked competitors, took a month of Blender and Unreal training, gathered feedback from existing users, then rebuilt the flow.

items.label: Decision
items.text: Redesign around a canvas-first 3D builder where users interact with scenes in real time — not a rigid, disconnected editor.

items.label: Surfaces
items.text: Dashboard, org management, 3D scene editor, object and environment settings, timeline, generation and export.

items.label: Outcome
items.text: A platform that turns scene-building into real, train-ready datasets — auto-annotated and ready to drop straight into a model pipeline.

items.label: Lesson
items.text: The best design comes from staying close to the real work users are trying to do.

sections.title: It worked. It was just rigid.
sections.subtitle: A working product that users couldn't move freely inside.
paragraphs: Terra already had a working version when I joined the project. The technology was sound — it generated synthetic data. But using it was the problem. Scene customisation was limited. There was no way to move the camera, no precise object placement, no way to preview how a scene would look under different lighting. Editing felt disconnected, and the platform didn't scale for teams.
My role as Product Owner and Designer was to redesign it — better usability, more features, and a real fix for the pain points users faced in the original.

sections.text: The product wasn't broken. The experience was. My job was to redesign the experience without breaking what worked.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Terra old version — the original interface ]

sections.title: I didn't design first. I learned first.
sections.subtitle: Benchmarks, then a month inside Blender and Unreal.
paragraphs: To know what to design, I studied the field. I benchmarked the leading synthetic data platforms — Synthesis AI, Anyverse, Bifrost AI, Parallel Domain — to understand where the industry bar sat and where Terra could go further.

But Terra runs on 3D workflows, and I couldn't design for something I didn't understand. So I took a month of Blender and Unreal Engine training with top creators — scene design, materials, lighting, animation. That month changed how I designed. I stopped guessing at what a 3D artist needed and started designing from how the work actually gets done.

sections.text: You can't design a 3D tool from the outside. I learned the craft first, then designed for it.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Benchmark board — competitor tools compared ]

sections.title: What the new Terra had to solve.
sections.subtitle: Straight from the people who used the old one.
paragraphs: I reached out to users of the previous version and gathered direct feedback. The same problems kept surfacing: limited scene customisation, unintuitive tools, minimal control over rendering, and poor scalability for teams.

I spent the following weeks turning that into direction — pulling best practices from the platforms and creative tools I'd studied, and building a new user flow that reflected the expanded feature set and fixed the old limitations. This phase wasn't just about refining the experience. It was about expanding what the product could be.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: New user flow — the redesigned journey ]

sections.title: Getting everyone to see it before building it.
sections.subtitle: Mid-fidelity proposal, early feedback, aligned direction.
paragraphs: Before touching high-fidelity design, I brought the research and early concepts to stakeholders. I walked them through the proposed user flows, showed how each feature answered a specific pain point from the old platform, and laid out the key improvements planned for the new version.

To make the direction tangible, I prepared a mid-fidelity proposal showing how the updated layout would look and work. It let stakeholders see the redesign clearly and give feedback early — before it was expensive to change.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Mid-fidelity proposal shown to stakeholders ]

sections.title: Same product. A frame that finally fits.
sections.subtitle: The redesign, told through the moments that define it.
paragraphs: With research, layout direction, and the revised flow aligned, I moved into high-fidelity design. Here are the moments that define how the new Terra works.

sections.title: Users used to lose track of their own work.
paragraphs: The old dashboard had no visual hierarchy and made resuming or managing multiple projects a chore. I rebuilt it around a visually rich project overview — recently opened projects shown as large, recognisable thumbnails with key metadata like last edit date and owner, so users can pick up where they left off and collaborate fast.

Navigation was the other pain point. Jumping between projects or finding admin tools felt clunky, so I restructured the sidebar and tab system for seamless workspace switching. Tools like project settings and org management sit contextually — there when you need them, out of the way when you don't.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Terra redesigned dashboard — project thumbnails, restructured sidebar ]

sections.title: No camera control. No precise placement. No lighting preview.
paragraphs: The old Builder was missing the basics users expected. Editing felt rigid and disconnected — hard to build anything dynamic or realistic. I reimagined the whole thing around a focused, canvas-based interface where users interact with their scenes in real time. Two panels carry most of the weight: Object Settings and Environment Settings.

sections.caption: Object Settings panel
sections.label: [ IMAGE: Object Settings panel ]

sections.caption: Environment Settings panel
sections.label: [ IMAGE: Environment Settings panel ]

sections.title: Two features define where the product is heading.
paragraphs: The redesign also brought in AI features I designed end-to-end. A third, Auto Segment Anything (ASA), handles automatic image segmentation and occlusion between real images and 3D objects — designed, but not covered in depth here.

sections.title: Two AI features, designed end-to-end.

items.title: Restyle (MAT)
items.text: Synthetic data often looks synthetic. MAT lets users upload a real-world reference image, and Terra extracts its visual characteristics — lighting, colour grading, noise, lens artifacts — and applies them to the synthetic output. Users control the strength and preview before exporting. The goal: datasets close enough to real that models actually generalise.

items.title: Sigma Agentic Builder (SAB)
items.text: AI-assisted scene building. Instead of placing every object by hand, users describe what they want and the system helps assemble it.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: MAT interface — upload reference, preview adaptation ]
[ IMAGE: SAB interface — if available ]

sections.title: From scene to train-ready data.
sections.subtitle: The whole point of Terra: usable ML data, not just pretty renders.
paragraphs: The generation flow ties it together. A user builds a scene, hits Generate, picks image or video, and the system processes the job in the background. A Work Orders panel tracks every job — in progress, completed, failed — so users can queue multiple generations and come back when they're ready.

What comes out is the payoff: datasets auto-annotated in COCO, YOLO, or Pascal VOC — labeled, structured, and ready to train on with no manual annotation step. That's the difference between a 3D render and a training asset.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Generate modal — image / video, generation settings ]
[ IMAGE: Work Orders panel — job tracking, download states ]
[ IMAGE: Received dataset — generated images with annotations/labels ]

sections.title: I took the dataset straight into Roboflow.
paragraphs: To prove the loop closes, I took a batch of Terra-generated images straight into Roboflow and trained a model on them — no real-world photos in the set. The dataset dropped in cleanly, annotations intact, and trained without manual cleanup. That's the whole thesis of the product in one test: build a scene, generate, train.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Roboflow — Terra dataset uploaded, training run on generated images ]

sections.text: A synthetic data platform is only as good as what a model can learn from it. The dataset going straight into Roboflow and training clean was the real proof.

sections.title: What this project taught me.

items.title: Learn the craft before you design for it.
items.text: A month in Blender and Unreal changed every decision that followed. You can't design a 3D tool well from the outside — I had to understand how scenes are actually built, lit, and rendered first.

items.title: The best direction comes from the old product's users.
items.text: The people who struggled with the original version told me exactly what to fix. Listening to the real work beat any assumption I could have made in Figma.

items.title: The dataset is the deliverable, not the scene.
items.text: It's easy to over-invest in how the editor looks and forget users come for train-ready data. Designing generation and export to end in clean, labeled output mattered as much as any editor panel.

items.title: Ownership means deciding what matters.
items.text: As Product Owner I defined the MVP, prioritised features, and shaped the vision from the ground up. That freedom only works if you stay disciplined about what actually earns its place.

sections.title: If I could go back.
paragraphs: I'd get real users into the redesigned Builder sooner. The research and stakeholder alignment were solid, but the canvas-first flow was something I mostly validated internally and in Figma. One session watching an engineer try to build a complex scene would have surfaced friction faster than another round of my own iteration.

And I'd document the design system earlier. With 35+ modules across a dashboard and a full 3D editor, the patterns I set up front held the redesign together — but I built a lot of it reactively. Formalising it sooner would have saved rework as the feature set expanded.

caseStudies.paletteLabel: Selling the art was never the hard part.
caseStudies.title: Selling the art was never the hard part.
caseStudies.blurb: Myanmar had artists with nowhere to sell and buyers with no reason to feel safe. 50+ interviews reframed the brief: Kanote wasn't an art shop — it was the first trust layer for a market that never had one.

detail.subtitle: Earning the trust was.
detail.summary: Myanmar had artists with nowhere to sell and buyers with no reason to feel safe. Fifty-plus interviews and gallery visits reframed the brief: Kanote wasn't an art shop — it was the first trust layer for a market that had never had one.

items.label: Voices in research
items.sub: Artists and art lovers interviewed 1:1, plus gallery owners and curators

items.label: Usability testers
items.sub: Whose feedback forced us to cut features, not add them

items.label: Core solutions
items.sub: Protected payments, a community feed, and a story behind every piece

sections.title: We set out to build a store. The research said build trust.
paragraphs: In a market unfamiliar with digital art platforms, the blocker was never listing artwork — it was confidence. Artists feared being unseen and underpaid; buyers feared paying for something they couldn't hold. Every core decision became an answer to one question: why should I trust this?

items.label: Problem
items.text: Myanmar artists had talent but no trusted platform; buyers had no safe, transparent way to purchase.

items.label: Method
items.text: 50+ one-on-one interviews, gallery visits with owners and curators, then a survey to verify the patterns at scale.

items.label: Decision
items.text: Design for trust and visibility first. Commerce follows confidence, not the other way around.

items.label: Surfaces
items.text: Protected payment paths, a community feed for discovery, and an artist-story panel on every artwork.

items.label: Outcome
items.text: A simpler, calmer platform — after usability testing made us cut the features we were proudest of.

items.label: Lesson
items.text: When users ask for more, the right answer is often less. Prioritisation is design.

sections.title: A market with no front door.
sections.subtitle: Plenty of art. Plenty of buyers. No trusted place for them to meet.
paragraphs: Myanmar's emerging artists had work to sell and no real platform for it — and buyers had no transparent, safe way to pay for something they couldn't physically inspect. The market had barely encountered a digital art platform, so we couldn't lean on habits that didn't exist. Building a storefront would have been easy. Building one people trusted enough to use was the real problem.

sections.text: The hard part wasn't listing artwork. It was giving two strangers a reason to believe the transaction.

sections.title: Fifty conversations and a gallery tour.
sections.subtitle: We went to where the art already lived — online and off.
paragraphs: We spoke with more than fifty artists and art lovers one-on-one, through social media, community networks, and in-person meetups. Then we left the screen — the team visited local galleries and sat with owners and curators who showed us how emerging artists really navigate the scene. Interviews gave depth; a structured survey across Myanmar verified the patterns at scale.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Research team visiting galleries & exhibitions ]
[ IMAGE: Survey responses ]

sections.text: Qualitative depth first, then quantitative proof. We refused to design on a vivid anecdote.

sections.title: Two users. One shared need.
sections.subtitle: Different goals, same blocker — trust.
paragraphs: The research resolved into two personas. The emerging artist needed visibility and fair, protected opportunities to sell. The passionate art lover needed a safe, transparent way to buy. Their goals diverged, but both pointed at the same missing ingredient: trust. From there we wrote sharp problem statements and translated them into user flows for browsing, uploading, buying on installments, and community.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: User personas ]
[ IMAGE: User flow — browse, upload, purchase, community ]

sections.title: Three answers to one question.
sections.subtitle: Every core feature was a reason to trust the platform.
paragraphs: We wireframed the four jobs that mattered — browse, buy, upload, connect — and iterated fast, killing anything unclear before it hardened. Then the visual language: bright and inviting, clean layouts, large touch targets, vibrant accents matching the creativity of the people using it. Three solutions did the heavy lifting, each answering the same question — why should I trust this?

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Low-fidelity wireframes ]

items.title: Two ways to pay — both protected.
items.text: Buyers choose Pay in Full or an installment plan, each governed by terms that protect artist and buyer alike. Flexibility for buyers; security for the artists selling.

items.title: A community, not a catalogue.
items.text: A feed where artists post work, share updates, and talk directly with collectors — turning a marketplace into a place where rising talent actually gets discovered.

items.title: Every artwork tells its story.
items.text: An About panel on each piece pairs the artist's story — background, inspiration, process — with hard specs like medium, materials, dimensions, and edition. Context plus facts, so buyers connect and commit.

sections.title: We built too much. Then we cut it.
sections.subtitle: Our proudest version was also our most overwhelming.
paragraphs: The first full build answered every request — too many features, too many paths, navigation that made first-time users freeze. We put the prototype in front of 15+ participants and watched the overwhelm in real time. Artists loved that Kanote handled delivery; buyers wanted clearer order tracking and richer profiles. So we did the harder thing than adding — we subtracted. Prioritisation simplified the layout, refined onboarding, and stripped friction from the core flows. The finished screens are calmer, and that calm is the result.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Usability testing — 15+ participants ]
[ IMAGE: Before / after — the simplified version ]

sections.text: The first build answered every request. That was exactly what was wrong with it.

sections.title: What this project taught me.

items.title: Commerce plus community is the product
items.text: Myanmar's artists didn't need a shop. They needed somewhere to be seen, heard, and supported — and the community turned out to be as load-bearing as the checkout.

items.title: Design for the local context
items.text: A market new to digital art needed trust, simplicity, and cultural fluency built in. Patterns borrowed wholesale from mature markets would have quietly missed.

items.title: Reading qualitative data is a skill
items.text: Fifty interviews are noise until you structure them. Affinity mapping turned scattered feedback into the empathy that drove the design.

items.title: Prioritisation is design
items.text: The strongest version of Kanote came from what we removed, not what we added. When everyone wants more, choosing less is the job.

sections.title: If I could go back.
paragraphs: I'd test for overwhelm earlier. We caught the feature bloat in usability testing — the right place, but late. A stricter prioritisation pass before the first full build would have spared a version we had to dismantle. And I'd close the loop with numbers: task success on the simplified flows, drop-off before and after the onboarding refinement, and completed purchases by payment type.

caseStudies.paletteLabel: From concept to working model, in one pipeline.
caseStudies.title: From concept to working model. In one pipeline.
caseStudies.metrics: Computer Vision · MLOps · Claude Agent SDK · 2025
caseStudies.blurb: A six-stage agent harness on the Claude Agent SDK that takes a CV model from raw images to a verified detector — with a human gate on every irreversible step. Proved it on a gaze-direction model at mAP@50 0.995.
caseStudies.mockLabel: [ CV Trainer — pipeline running end-to-end, human-gate prompts visible ]

detail.summary: Training a computer vision model means chaining together a stack of disconnected tools — data hosting, labeling, dataset versioning, training compute, evaluation, and deployment — and manually shepherding data through all of them. One wrong move corrupts the dataset. One credit overspend kills a training run mid-way. I built a harness that automates the whole chain while keeping a human in the loop at every step that matters.
detail.subtitle: Building a human-in-the-loop CV training harness with the Claude Agent SDK.

items.label: Stack
items.value: Claude Agent SDK · Roboflow · Ultralytics · Kaggle

items.label: Pipeline stages
items.sub: Intake → Label → Version → Train → Evaluate → Deploy

items.label: Training backends
items.sub: Roboflow cloud, local Apple MPS, and free Kaggle GPU — the user picks at the gate

items.label: MCP tools
items.sub: Everything the agent can reach: project ops, auto-label, export, train, evaluate, predict

sections.title: One conversation. One working detector.
paragraphs: Training CV models is repetitive, error-prone, and tool-hopping. Data lives in Roboflow. Training runs in the cloud — or on your laptop — or on Kaggle. Evaluation is a CSV file you have to parse yourself. I built a six-stage agent harness on the Claude Agent SDK that automates all of it, pausing for human approval before anything irreversible. The final proof: a gaze-direction detector for paralysis patients, trained end-to-end through the harness, that hit mAP@50 = 0.995.

items.label: Problem
items.text: Training a CV model chains together data hosting, labeling, versioning, training, and evaluation — all in separate tools, all manual, all easy to break.

items.label: Method
items.text: A six-stage agent pipeline on the Claude Agent SDK: one lean agent per stage, a human gate on every consequential action, Roboflow as the managed data layer.

items.label: Decision
items.text: Separate the stages cleanly. Each agent knows only its own tools. Routing, memory, and approvals live outside the agent — in the orchestrator and the gate.

items.label: Surfaces
items.text: CLI entry point, stage-by-stage prompting, COCO auto-labeler, 3-way training backend picker, append-only run ledger.

items.label: Outcome
items.text: Runs end-to-end on real data. Gaze-direction eye-tracking model: mAP@50 0.995, precision 0.993, recall 0.990 — trained entirely through the harness.

items.label: Lesson
items.text: Fine-tuning only earns its keep on custom classes with real labels. If the class is already in COCO, use the off-the-shelf model directly.

sections.title: Training a model is a stack of manual steps. All of them breakable.
paragraphs: The tools exist. Roboflow hosts datasets. Ultralytics trains detectors. Kaggle gives free GPU. But none of them talk to each other, and every hand-off is a place where something goes wrong.

You upload images, switch to a labeling tool, come back to generate a dataset version, export it, copy files into a training script, wait for training to finish, parse a CSV for metrics, and then manually decide whether the model is good enough to deploy. Miss one step — wrong augmentation schema, wrong split, wrong model format — and the whole run is wasted.

The other problem: no guardrails. A Roboflow training run burns credits. A bad dataset version gets frozen and used for training. There's no approval step between “I think this is ready” and “I just spent compute on garbage data.”

sections.text: The tools work individually. The problem is the hand-offs — and the complete absence of a human checkpoint before anything irreversible happens.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: The disconnected manual chain — Roboflow → label tool → version → training script → CSV → model ]

sections.title: I mapped the full chain before writing a line of agent code.
paragraphs: Before building anything, I scoped every step a CV practitioner actually goes through: image intake, annotation, versioning, augmentation, training, evaluation, deployment. Each step had a tool. Each tool had a different interface and a different failure mode.

I spent time understanding the Claude Agent SDK — how tool scoping works with disallowed_tools versus tools=, how can_use_tool gates fit into the agent loop, how setting_sources=[] keeps stray config files from bloating every request. I also dug into the Roboflow SDK — and found seven bugs in the process, all of which had to be fixed before the pipeline could work on real data.

sections.text: The Roboflow SDK had seven bugs the docs didn't mention. Real-data testing found all of them. You can't build a reliable pipeline on top of untested assumptions about your dependencies.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: The 7 Roboflow SDK bugs discovered and fixed ]

sections.title: What the harness had to guarantee.
paragraphs: After mapping the chain and testing the SDK, the requirements became clear.

notes.title: Human approval on side effects
notes.text: Every consequential action — creating a project, uploading images, freezing a dataset version, starting a training run — had to pause for human approval. No silent side effects.

notes.title: Stage isolation
notes.text: The label agent shouldn't be able to accidentally trigger a training run. Tool scope had to be enforced at the framework level, not just by prompting the model to behave.

notes.title: It had to work for free
notes.text: Roboflow's free tier allows ~2 training runs. The pipeline needed real alternatives: local training on Apple MPS and a Kaggle free GPU backend, so a user with no credits could still complete the loop.

notes.title: Honest auto-labeling
notes.text: A COCO-pretrained model can label the ~80 common classes. For anything else, the pipeline hands off to a manual seed loop — and says so clearly rather than silently failing.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Pipeline diagram — the six stages with gate markers ]

sections.title: One agent per stage. One gate per consequential action.
paragraphs: The harness splits cleanly into three layers.

Tools (tools/) are the capability layer — what can be done. roboflow_client.py wraps the Roboflow SDK with all seven bugs fixed. trainers.py holds the local and Kaggle training backends. autolabel.py runs COCO auto-labeling and writes Pascal VOC annotation files. cv_tools.py wraps all of it into 17 in-process MCP tools and builds the server the agents mount.

Agents (agents/) are the orchestration layer — how it's driven. stages.py defines one Stage per pipeline step: its allowed tools, its read-only subset, and its system prompt. orchestrator.py builds lean ClaudeAgentOptions per stage — scoping tools with disallowed_tools, disabling built-ins, wiring the gate. gate.py is the human checkpoint: a can_use_tool callback that surfaces four tools for approval (create_project, upload_images, generate_version, train) and auto-approves everything else. ledger.py writes every run to an append-only markdown file.
Reference (reference/) is the knowledge layer — what good looks like. Domain knowledge on augmentation strategy, dataset auditing, and evaluation thresholds is quoted directly into stage prompts.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Architecture diagram — tools / agents / reference layers ]

sections.title: The pipeline, told through each step.
paragraphs: With architecture defined and tools verified on real data, the pipeline runs as six sequential agent sessions.

sections.title: Stage by stage.

items.title: Intake
items.text: Understands the goal, picks the task type (detection / classification / segmentation), and reuses or creates a Roboflow project. Image upload is gated; the agent reports image and class counts before handing off.

items.title: Label
items.text: Two paths. For COCO classes, the auto-labeler drafts boxes on 5 sample images for review before committing to the full set. For custom classes, it routes to a seed loop: hand-label a few → quick-train → pre-label the rest. Upload is gated either way.

items.title: Version
items.text: Freezes the dataset with deterministic preprocessing (auto-orient + resize) and task-aware augmentation. It refuses to version an empty or unlabeled dataset. generate_version is gated.

items.title: Train
items.text: At the train gate the user picks a backend — Roboflow cloud, local Apple MPS, or Kaggle free GPU. Each has its own pre-flight checks and status polling.

items.title: Evaluate — the Stop Gate
items.text: Reads metrics (mAP@IoU, precision, recall, per-class), interprets them against an acceptance threshold, and runs the Stop Gate: PASS → deploy, FAIL → pick a fix and route back. The human makes the final call; the agent doesn't rubber-stamp.

items.title: Deploy
items.text: Spot-checks the trained model on real images, confirms predictions match the eval verdict, and marks it verified — or routes back if predictions look wrong.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Auto-label sample check — five images with boxes, approve / reject prompt ]

sections.title: Three training backends, one gate.

items.title: Roboflow
items.text: Cloud GPU on Roboflow credits (free tier ≈ 2 runs). A pre-flight credit check runs first. Non-blocking start, then poll train_status.

items.title: Local
items.text: Ultralytics YOLOv8 on Apple MPS or CPU. Completely free. Exports the frozen version, trains in-process, returns best.pt. Blocking until done.

items.title: Kaggle
items.text: Free cloud GPU. Pushes a private kernel that pulls the Roboflow version, trains with Ultralytics, writes best.pt. Polls kaggle_status, fetches weights with a 3-retry download.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Train gate prompt — backend picker in the terminal ]
[ IMAGE: Evaluate output — metrics table and Stop Gate verdict ]

sections.title: Eye-tracking for paralysis patients. mAP@50 = 0.995.
paragraphs: The harness was tested on a human-detection dataset first — 921 images, COCO auto-labeled. The pipeline ran end-to-end, but the resulting model was useless: the person class is already in COCO, so fine-tuning a tiny auto-labeled set just distilled and degraded the base model. The lesson was important — auto-labeling a COCO class on a small dataset trains fine, it just can't win.

The second test changed everything. The dataset: a gaze-direction eye-tracking set for wheelchair-assisted paralysis patients — six custom classes (Close, Down, Left, Right, Straight, Up), 210 training / 60 validation / 31 test images, all human-made labels. COCO has no concept of gaze direction. This was exactly the case the harness was built for.

The model passed the Stop Gate and was verified on test images through the deploy stage. A live webcam demo runs on top of the weights — webcam_eye_demo.py runs the gaze detector in real time and prints the control signal (Left / Right / Up / Down / Straight / Close) to stdout.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Terminal — evaluate output, Stop Gate PASS, per-class metrics ]
[ IMAGE: Webcam demo — live gaze detection with bounding box and class label ]

sections.text: The harness didn't just work. It proved the thesis: fine-tuning earns its keep on custom, non-COCO classes with real human-made labels — and for those cases it reaches near-perfect accuracy.

sections.title: What this project taught me.

items.title: Test on real data, not on assumptions.
items.text: Seven SDK bugs stayed hidden until real images and real API calls surfaced them. No amount of reading the docs would have found them. The test run was the only reliable audit.

items.title: Scope tools at the framework level, not in the prompt.
items.text: Telling an agent “only use these tools” in a system prompt is advisory. Using disallowed_tools in ClaudeAgentOptions is enforced. One is a suggestion; the other is a constraint.

items.title: The human gate is the product.
items.text: The gate is what makes this a harness rather than a script. Every consequential action pauses for confirmation — and that pause is where the human's domain knowledge enters the loop.

items.title: Fine-tuning has a clear decision rule.
items.text: If the class is in COCO, use the base model. If the class is custom and the labels are real, fine-tune. The eye-tracking result (0.995) versus person-detection (unusable) is the clearest illustration.

sections.title: If I could go back.
paragraphs: I'd fix the Roboflow SDK bugs earlier — before designing around assumptions about how the API behaved. Finding seven bugs mid-build meant re-routing the pipeline multiple times. A small upfront script exercising every API call on a throwaway project would have surfaced them in an hour.

And I'd build the Kaggle backend last, not third. The P100 / torch CUDA incompatibility, the phone-verification requirement, the transient download breaks — every Kaggle-specific failure mode added surface area before the core pipeline was solid. Local-first, then hosted, then Kaggle as a bonus would have been a cleaner build order.

caseStudies.title: Fifteen minutes before the range opens.
caseStudies.blurb: A Training Resource Management System for the Singapore Armed Forces — booking, asset issue, and trainee onboarding designed around the fifteen minutes before a live range opens, for four roles on one shared model.
caseStudies.mockLabel: [ TRMS — the operator's home screen, fifteen minutes before a range opens ]

detail.summary: SAF marksmanship and specialised-weapon training runs on a precise schedule, but the operations around it ran on institutional memory — bookings in spreadsheets, onboarding on clipboards, assets tracked by whoever was on duty. TRMS replaces that with one shared system: booking, asset issue, and trainee onboarding designed around the fifteen minutes before a live range opens, with role-specific surfaces for operators, instructors, admins, and maintainers. I led the design end-to-end, grounded in field research across multiple camps.
detail.subtitle: Designing a training-operations platform for the fifteen minutes before a live range opens.

items.label: User roles
items.sub: System Admin, Operator, Instructor, Maintainer — one shared model, four surfaces

items.label: Operational flows
items.sub: Booking → Assignment → Onboarding, each carrying state to the next

items.label: Training systems
items.sub: IMT (Individual Marksmanship) and SWT (Specialised Weapon) on one platform

sections.title: Institutional memory doesn't survive a shift change.
paragraphs: SAF training is precise — rounds fired, drills timed, scores logged. The operations around it weren't: bookings in spreadsheets, onboarding on clipboards, asset issue tracked by whoever was on duty. When the operator changed mid-rotation, half the state changed with them. TRMS makes the state travel with the session instead of the person — one shared model, role-specific surfaces, and an interface fast enough to beat paper in the fifteen minutes before a range opens. I designed the whole loop, grounded in field research across multiple camps.

items.label: Problem
items.text: Training operations ran on institutional memory — bookings, onboarding, and asset issue scattered across paper and disconnected systems, with state dying at every shift change.

items.label: Method
items.text: Field research across multiple camps — watching operators run Built-In Tests, handovers, and mid-session maintenance — instead of writing a spec from HQ.

items.label: Decision
items.text: One shared data model with role-specific surfaces. Same booking record; four different views. Extending to a new role becomes a view problem, not a data-model one.

items.label: Surfaces
items.text: Booking creation, assignment (RFID asset issue), trainee onboarding, and role-specific home screens for Admin, Operator, Instructor, and Maintainer.

items.label: Outcome
items.text: Shipped April 2025 across SAF camps — IMT and SWT programmes on one platform, integrating IOS, ATMS, and RFID.

items.label: Lesson
items.text: Accessible design for careful, time-pressured operators is just good design — larger targets, plainer language, and fewer traps made the system faster for everyone.

sections.title: Training operations run on institutional memory.
sections.subtitle: And memory is the wrong currency when the range opens at 0800.
paragraphs: SAF marksmanship and specialised-weapon training runs on a fixed schedule, across multiple subsystems — the Instructor Operating Station, the Army Training Management System, RFID asset tracking, booking, onboarding, Built-In Tests. The training itself is precise: rounds fired, drills timed, scores logged, weapons accounted for. The operations around it were not. Bookings sat in spreadsheets. Onboarding happened on clipboards. Asset issue was tracked by whoever was on duty. If the operator on duty changed mid-rotation, half the state changed with them.

TRMS had to close three gaps at once, for the operators, instructors, admins, and maintainers running these programmes across multiple camps on tight rotation. My job was to design the whole loop — from booking through session close-out — and make it something an operator with fifteen minutes to prep a range can actually run, not something they route around under pressure.

notes.title: Coordination gap
notes.text: Bookings, onboarding, asset issue, and session state each lived in their own system or on paper. State didn't travel between them, so every handoff was where accountability dropped.

notes.title: Role misalignment
notes.text: Admin, Operator, Instructor, and Maintainer each need a different slice of the same reality. The old tools gave everyone the same generic view — or forced each role into a separate silo.

notes.title: Operator accessibility
notes.text: The people running sessions are career military — fast and competent, but under real time pressure. A system that demands digital fluency or fine motor control is a system that gets skipped.

sections.text: Institutional memory isn't a workflow. It's a person who might not be there tomorrow.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: The gap — training time on the schedule vs training time on the range ]

sections.title: You don't design a training system from a spec sheet.
paragraphs: We couldn't write a useful requirements document from HQ. The gap between how the work is supposed to happen and how it actually happens only shows up when you're standing next to the operator at 0730 with a range opening in half an hour and one trainee's ID card missing.

So my team and I went to the camps — multiple camps, across weeks. I watched Operators run Built-In Tests at 0700, Instructors take handovers, Maintainers walk in mid-session because a base station wouldn't calibrate. I asked questions that would have sounded dumb from a distance — “why do you write it here first and then move it there?” — and every one uncovered a step the documented process pretended didn't exist.

notes.title: The paper path is the real path
notes.text: When the pressure was on, people fell back to paper — it doesn't crash, doesn't need a login, doesn't hide state behind a modal. Anything digital had to be at least as fast as paper at the point of first use.

notes.title: Handovers are where state dies
notes.text: Every shift change was a small crisis. Whatever the system remembered, it had to remember visibly — so no state lived only in an operator's head.

notes.title: Careful, not slow
notes.text: Older operators didn't need pity accessibility. They needed a system that didn't punish carefulness with hidden delete buttons, tiny confirm dialogs, or ambiguous icons.

sections.text: The spec is what someone in an office wants to be true. The camp is what's actually true.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Two workflows side by side — the documented process and the one operators actually run, with the paper-trail bridge between them ]

sections.title: A shared system is not the same as a shared screen.
paragraphs: Four roles worked in the same TRMS at different stakes. The trap is one of two failure modes: build one interface for all four and it serves none — every role wading through fields they don't need; or build four separate apps and now the underlying state is duplicated four ways and nobody knows which copy is true.

I designed for a shared model with role-specific surfaces. One booking record; four different views of it. The Operator sees the check-in queue, the Instructor the assessment sheet, the Maintainer the hardware assigned to the session, the Admin the audit trail. Same record, different questions asked of it — and only the fields relevant to that question on screen. Onboarding a new user type later became a view problem, not a data-model problem.

sections.title: The four roles.

items.title: System Admin
items.text: Owns configuration, permissions, and the shape of the system. Low time pressure.

items.title: Operator
items.text: Runs the day — books sessions, onboards trainees, issues weapons, closes out.

items.title: Instructor
items.text: Watches trainee performance and reviews outcomes. Decisions during and after the session, not before.

items.title: Maintainer
items.text: Keeps the hardware alive. Appears exactly when something is broken and everything has stopped.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: A single booking record at the centre, with four cards showing what each role sees ]

sections.title: Accessibility isn't a category of user. It's a category of moment.
paragraphs: The research kept surfacing one scenario: an Operator, uniform on, fifteen minutes before a range opens, needs to onboard a group of trainees, run the Built-In Test on the base stations, and confirm assignments. Everything else in the brief was downstream of surviving that moment. So the interface principles weren't clean, intuitive, delightful — they were operational.

notes.title: Everything visible without scrolling
notes.text: If the Operator has to hunt for a core action, the paper form wins.

notes.title: Touch targets for gloved hands
notes.text: Some sites are outdoor; gloves are not optional.

notes.title: Contrast that survives midday glare
notes.text: The design system had to work at 90% brightness with the sun in the wrong place.

notes.title: Errors in the operator's language
notes.text: Not “validation failed on field X” but “IMT-03 is inactive. Assign to a different pair?”

notes.title: Confirm the destructive, not the reversible
notes.text: Every unnecessary confirm dialog trains the user to click through the necessary one.

sections.text: Fast for the operator isn't fast for the animation. It's fast for the hand.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Anatomy of the Operator's home screen — hit targets highlighted, primary actions labelled by the seconds they save ]

sections.title: Three flows that carry state.
sections.subtitle: A workflow is only as good as its worst handoff.
paragraphs: The three flows that made or broke the system were booking creation, assignment creation, and trainee onboarding — each a stage in one continuous loop, not an independent form. The principle across all three: state travels with the session, not with the person running it. When a shift changed, the incoming Operator opened the same session and saw exactly what the previous one saw.

sections.title: Booking → Assignment → Onboarding.

items.title: Booking creation
items.text: Individual or group session, date, time, camp, station pair. Base stations are paired (IMT-03 + IMT-04) and both must be active. Committing a booking pre-allocates the stations and marks them unavailable. The system enforces the pairing, so the Operator doesn't have to remember it.

items.title: Assignment creation
items.text: Allocates the physical assets — weapons, magazines, RFID-tagged items — to the session. Scan the tag, the system fills in the metadata, confirm with one tap. Chain-of-custody is the speed, because scan-and-confirm replaced the manual write-down.

items.title: Trainee onboarding
items.text: Check-in turns the booking into a live session. Trainees matched against the roster, assets confirmed, handoff to the IOS. Every trainee's state is one of five explicit statuses; the session can't close with any ambiguous.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: The three flows as a linear loop — Booking → Assignment → Onboarding → Session → Close-out — with state labelled on the arrows ]

sections.title: One platform, two training systems, three contracts to honour.
paragraphs: TRMS shipped as a modular platform covering both IMT (Individual Marksmanship Trainer) and SWT (Specialised Weapon Trainer) programmes across SAF camps. Shipping wasn't just delivering an interface — it was honouring three integrations that each pulled the design in a different direction.

sections.title: Three contracts to honour.

items.title: IOS — Instructor Operating Station
items.text: On onboarding, TRMS hands state to IOS, where the Instructor runs the drill and captures scores; when IOS closes the session, TRMS finalises assets and records. Every field surfaced during onboarding is one IOS needs at that exact moment.

items.title: ATMS — Army Training Management System
items.text: Upstream. Course schedules and rosters flow in; completion records flow out. TRMS accepts ATMS as the source of truth for who's supposed to train — and reconciles it against who actually showed up.

items.title: RFID — the hardware layer
items.text: Every weapon, magazine, and tagged asset is scanned through TRMS. Chain-of-custody survives from issue to return with no manual write-down. RFID doesn't wait, and neither can the interface layered on it.

items.label: Role types with distinct permission scopes

sections.label: [ IMAGE: The system boundary — TRMS at the centre, IOS / ATMS / RFID as the three contracts, four roles as the surfaces above ]

sections.title: What I learned.

items.title: Field research is a stance, not a phase.
items.text: Every time I thought I understood the workflow well enough to skip a camp visit, I got something wrong. The Operators saved me more times than the spec did.

items.title: “Accessible design” is often just design.
items.text: Larger targets, plainer language, fewer traps — the choices for older, less digital-native Operators made the system faster for every user. There's no accessibility tax when the constraint is real.

items.title: Hardware integration is a design constraint.
items.text: RFID, base-station pairing, and IOS handoff meant the UI had physical latencies to respect. A spinner wasn't a pattern — it was an honest signal that the base station was still calibrating.

items.title: Role-specific surfaces on a shared model scale.
items.text: Duplicated systems don't. Made early, this decision kept paying dividends every time a new role or subsystem needed a view onto the same data.

items.title: Military users are rigorous users.
items.text: They notice inconsistency and ambiguity, and they'll route around one confusing icon forever. Designing for them raised my bar for what “clear” means.

sections.title: In active use across SAF camps.
paragraphs: IMT TRMS shipped in April 2025 and is in active use across SAF training camps.

moreProjects.title: The ops table that runs itself
moreProjects.blurb: A spreadsheet-grade table for non-technical teams — sorting, bulk edits, and audit trails baked in.

moreProjects.title: Unified design tokens
moreProjects.blurb: One token pipeline feeding Figma, web, and native — themes flip without a single hand-edit.

writing.title: Designing trust into autonomous interfaces
writing.excerpt: When software acts on its own, the UI's job is to make the seams legible.

writing.title: The design system is the product
writing.excerpt: Why tokens, not screens, are the real deliverable.

writing.title: Shipping is a design decision
writing.excerpt: A case for designers who hold the keyboard, not just the canvas.

bullets: Spent 1.5 year designing the end-to-end flow of IMT TRMS, a training resource management system for MINDEF Singapore
Shipped across defence, education, computer vision tooling, and physical security — four domains in one company
Promoted to Product Owner/Designer — redesigned both Terra (World Builder) and Accel (Model Validator) from scratch
Built and delivered the design system used across all products
Picked up computer vision along the way and started contributing to CV-related tasks across products
Designed the entire Pistil healthcare platform — user, admin, and doctor dashboards plus the mobile app
Started every feature in Low-Fi wireframes before touching Hi-Fi
Let prototypes do the talking in dev handoffs — less confusion, fewer surprises
Designed two products from the ground up — Global Caring, a nursing platform, and Sofinise, a crypto payment gateway
Worked directly with developers and stakeholders on both projects
Worked alongside senior UX designers from Myanmar on client projects
Main project was Jar Aye, an Olympiad math platform for young learners
Followed the full design workflow — research, wireframes, prototyping, testing, and handoff
First full-time design role. Mostly consumer products — blogging apps, ride-hailing, and other mini-apps
Tried to ship a product every month, which meant learning to move fast without cutting corners
Edit and publish video and podcast content for UXMM events across YouTube and Instagram
Manage the org's media library
Interviewed 50+ artists from local Myanmar art communities to understand their needs and pain points
Ran user research and built wireframes with a cross-functional team
Designed Kanote, a digital art marketplace for Myanmar artists
Built a blog website for the organisation from scratch
Learned layout fundamentals and design tooling from a senior web design instructor
Worked with other UI designers on interface designs
Got to see how different designers approach the same problem — picked up habits I still use

contributions.title: RIC Applied AI Innovation Challenge (AIIC 2026)
contributions.description: AI hackathon at Rangsit University. Built a working prototype in a team of four, pitched it live to judges. Won Second Runner-Up.

contributions.title: Falling Walls Lab 2023
contributions.description: 3-day programme on pitching research-backed ideas. My topic was Breaking the Wall of Art Community Barriers — an early version of what later became Kanote. Didn't make the finals, but it was where I first had to frame a design idea as a business case.

contributions.title: Techstars Startup Weekend Yangon 2023
contributions.description: 3-day startup challenge. Built the pitch deck for Kanote Art Platform and presented it to judges and investors.

contributions.title: Iterate UX Research Challenge
contributions.description: 6-week UXR project for CookieYes, a cookie consent platform. Ran the full research cycle and presented findings to a judging panel.

contributions.title: Iterate UX Design Challenge
contributions.description: 6-week UX design competition. Ideated, prototyped, and tested a solution as a team, then pitched it to judges.
